title: Mangazine Evangelion Review Circa May 1996
wikitext:
[]: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Nov 09, 2020 10:50 am A friend of mine stumbled across some old 90's fan magazines at a local comic book shop and got the Evangelion/Hideaki Anno centered issues for me a couple years back. I've been slowly reading through them, and found some interesting stuff! Back in the pre/early internet days, many of the fandom communicated through inexpensive, fan-published magazines. This is basically the internet before the internet! Many of these were printed on newspaper print, with only the outer cover displaying any color images. One of these magazines was called "Mangazine," published by Antarctic Press. In their May 1996 issue, Mangazine featured a review written by Douglas Dlin and Mie Kominami, for Neon Genesis Evangelion , which wouldn't even begin releasing in the US until August of that year. I found this review interesting as it was published before the Japanese release of Evangelion: Death & Rebirth or The End of Evangelion , which means that this review of the series based entirely on the "EoTV" ending and the promise of of an extension of the story! I'm posting the text from that article below (makes it easier to quote), as well as some scans of the article in the spoiler tags. 1996 Evangelion Review  SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by Line » Mon Nov 09, 2020 11:00 am This look great as f#ck! Would have loved to own one and reread in nostalgia in the present day, from time to time

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Nov 09, 2020 1:05 pm I was too young to have grown up with these publications back in 1996 (I was 9 years old at the time), but they are fun to read through and see what everyone thought of Eva at the time. I definitely feel that same palpable anticipation regarding the last Evangelion movie installment that the audience did back in 1996 for End of Evangelion as I feel now for Shin Evangelion .

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by Line » Mon Nov 09, 2020 2:52 pm Well not me. No offence to you'r excitation, but personally, I feel unexcited as fuck I have a pernicious and well ancred feeling (conviction, even) that's this movie is going to be shit. Atleast for me. The only thing I'm excited about is, from Rei ":o" cat face as people call it, and how she extends her hand to someone (Shinji?) is that's she going to have character developpement linked OR NOT(!!) to Rei II. And even to Lilith and being a clone, why not! I've have enough of the pattern of whenever she start developp as individual, bam she die ! Rei II in the tv serie, starting developping as an individual=died. Rei III/as Lilith=Stayed a puppet but might have come trough some sort of epiphany during being Lilith/instrumentality about her individuality and her own humanity her link with others=Died. Rei II in rebuild=Started to develop some sort of common, and good individuality being more sociable in her own shy, introverted way=Died. -.-

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by Berserker » Tue Nov 10, 2020 4:59 am FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Were the racial tensions and civil wars always a thing in the lore and I just missed it?

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Nov 10, 2020 6:29 am ^ Spoiling episode 1 was probably expected and appreciated, since one of those subbed anime tapes would cost about a full day's wage on minimum wages in the US back in 1996. Many people just didn't have access to watch a couple episodes before buying the show, so knowing as much as you can to see if you'd like it is probably a smart thing to do on a budget like that. Each tape would have two episodes, so there's still plenty of unspoiled material on the tape if Episode 2 remained unspoiled. I don't know if it was weird that he gave the propaganda backstory inasmuch as he gave any sort of backstory to that show. The tension between the fake history lesson and Second Impact is probably something you'd want the viewer to discover on their own, so even mentioning either of those is weird to me.

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by Berserker » Tue Nov 10, 2020 7:02 am The tension between the fake history lesson and Second Impact is probably something you'd want the viewer to discover on their own

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Mon Nov 16, 2020 8:30 am Pedantic me finds it mildly amusing to see all the little errors and inaccuracies that riddle the pages (Shinji's in Eva-01, for Yui-sama's sake, not Eva-02!) and the antiquarian me finds this incredibly interesting.

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by IgRAzm » Tue Nov 17, 2020 9:32 am @UrsusArctos right, it also stood out to me how they almost frame it like Shinji's synchronisation with his Eva works like telepathy/telekinesis, when it protected him from the falling debris.

[]: Re: "Mangazine" Evangelion Review, Circa May 1996             Quote Post by Lavinius » Tue Nov 17, 2020 11:29 am FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: The review calls back to reports they did for issue 39, so now I have to track that one down. I appreciate how even though reviewer realized that ridiculing Shinji for not "getting in the f*cking robot" was dumb. Also, I'm gonna be honest, I was just as zoned out during the teacher's lesson propaganda as the rest of the students were in the actual show. Were the racial tensions and civil wars always a thing in the lore and I just missed it?

Article: Mangazine Evangelion Review Circa May 1996
 back to reports they did for issue 39, so now I have to track that one down. I appreciate how even though reviewer realized that ridiculing Shinji for not "getting in the f*cking robot" was dumb. Also, I'm gonna be honest, I was just as zoned out during the teacher's lesson propaganda as the rest of the students were in the actual show. Were the racial tensions and civil wars always a thing in the lore and I just missed it?
